title: practical english usage - Part 151
content:
In a formal style, most can be used to mean ‘ very’ .

This is the most extraordinary day of my life.

Which part of the concert did you like most?

and adverbs (►  204).

most Most is the superlative of much,  and is used to form superlative adjectives low Low is an adjective and adverb (a low bridge, a low voice, bend low).

Don't talk so loud(ly) - you'll wake the whole street.

loud Loud is often used informally as an adverb after a verb.

/ hate arriving late. I haven't been to the theatre much  lately.

‘ recently’ . Compare: late The  adverb late has a similar meaning to the adjective late; lately means He was Justly punished for his crimes.

just, meaning 'in accordance with justice or the law'; the adverb is justly.

just )ust is an adverb with several meanings (►  503). There is also an adjective Throw it as high as you can.    I can highly recommend it.

- He can jump  really high.    - It's highly amusing.

(it often means ‘ very much ’ ). Compare: high High refers to height; highly (rather formal) expresses an extreme degree For hardly. . . when in clauses of time, ►    480.   For hardly any, ever, etc, ►     366.3.

Anna works hard. Her brother hardly works.

I've hardly got any clean clothes left.

Hardly means ‘ almost not'.

Hit it hard. I trained really hard for the marathon.

hard The  adverb hard means 'with a lot of force, energetically'.

You can speak freely -1 won't tell anyone what you say.

You can eat free in my restaurant whenever you like.

‘ without limit or restriction’ . Compare: free The  adverb free (used after a verb) means ‘ without payment’ ; freely means on a note that is too low'). In most other cases, the adverb is flatly.

flat Flat can be used as an adverb in a musical sense (to sing flat means ‘ to sing ɑ   finely tuned engine finely chopped onions (= cut up very small) similar ideas.

The  adverb finely is used to talk about small careful adjustments and That suits me fine. You're doing fine.

fine The  adverb fine (= well) is used in some informal expressions.

remove' in expressions like hold fast, stick fast, fast colours.

‘ completely' in the expression fast asleep, and it means 'tight', ‘ impossible to fast Fast can mean both ‘ quick' and ‘ quickly' (a fast car goes fast). Fast means grammar •  194   adverbs or adjectives: confusing cases style. Typical expressions: hold tight, packed tight (compare tightly packed).

tight After a verb, tight can be used instead of tightly, especially in an informal Surely you're not going out in that old coat?

Surely house prices will stop rising soon!

Surely (not) is used to express opinions or surprise (►   600 for details).

'Can I borrow your tennis racket? 'Sure.' American English.

sure Sure is often used to mean 'certainly' in an informal style, especially in from one place to another.

straight The  adverb and the adjective are the same. A  straight road goes straight cases, soundly is used (e.g. She's sleeping soundly).

sound Sound is used as an adverb in the expression sound asleep. In other informally after go and some other verbs. Examples: go slow, drive slow.

slow Slow is used as an adverb in road signs (e.g. SLOW - DANGEROUS BEND), and impatient way of speaking.

and cut short (= 'interrupt'). Shortly means 'soon'; it can also describe an short Short is used as an adverb in the expressions stop short (= 'stop suddenly') I thought you spoke to her rather sharply.

She looked at him  sharply.

In other senses the adverb is sharply.

(meaning 'with a big change of direction').

high'), and is used in the expressions turn sharp left and turn sharp right It also has a musical sense (to sing sharp means 'to sing on a note that is too Can you be there at six o'clock sharp?

sharp Sharp can be used as an adverb to mean 'punctually'.

It serves you right. (... rightly is not possible.) I rightly assumed that Henry was not coming. You guessed right.

verbs, and is usually informal. Compare: Right and rightly can both be used to mean 'correctly'. Right is only used after Turn the gas right down.

The snowball hit me right on the nose.
